# Transcrição: 01- Course 5 - Finding The Deals

- **Fonte:** 01- Course 5 - Finding The Deals
- **Duração:** 06:59
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

So let's talk about finding deals. Look, you're not going to make any money until you lock down a deal and find a deal. That being said, don't buy the wrong deal because you'll violate the first rule of investing, which is don't lose money. Before you lock down a deal, up to that point, it's either just research with the intent to make it a career or it's just a hobby. To get there, you have to find deals, which means you need to be looking for deals every single day and I cannot emphasize this enough. It's a big part of the reason you need to go where you know, that is, your backyard. The first market I ever shopped, the place where I learned and didn't make any money, but actually set up my career was where I lived in Houston, Texas. I was 29, maybe 30 years old and I started shopping real estate every single weekend. This is after I made that first single family mistake, that purchase. I shopped deals every weekend for three years. I exhausted every broker in the market and I knew about every deal on the market, those that were off market deals, those that were on market deals, and even I had researched deals that weren't for sale. Even so, I got a great education despite the fact I'd never bought a deal. I learned the difference between good deals and bad deals. I became familiar with what a good deal looked like, what it felt like, what it looked like when you walked on it, parked in front of it. When you looked at the financial statements, I knew I could feel what a good deal was versus a bad deal. I missed out on major opportunity. I could have made a lot of money there and it wasn't long after leaving that market that I realized how much money I would have made had I just taken a leap and started investing, but I didn't. And to this day, this massive missed opportunity still creeps around in the back of my mind knowing what a good deal looks like. And when I pass, by the way, I have lost more money not buying deals than anything compared to losing money buying deals because I've never lost money buying a deal. Now, I can't promise that you're not going to lose money buying a deal, but I can promise you this. You're not going to make money in real estate until you buy a deal. I pushed ahead. I moved to San Diego to expand my other businesses and because I thought that the lifestyle there was desirable. When I moved to San Diego and once I got my other businesses established, I started shopping apartments in the San Diego market. I was driving through the city and I said, dude, I got that feeling. I got that same feeling that I had when I was in Houston shopping apartments knowing, knowing that I didn't buy anything. I remember telling myself not long after I left Houston, if I ever have that sensation again, that feeling of value, this is the right time, the economy set up perfectly,

## [03:02 - 06:02]

the rental market set up perfectly. I know the market. If I ever get that feeling again, I said to myself, I will make a purchase. It wasn't long after I was in San Diego. Literally months I had been shopping for real estate in the San Diego market. And driving in downtown with Dale Christensen, I looked at him and said, man, I got that feeling. This time though, I had high confidence, sky high confidence, complete confidence of knowing the market, knowing the rental income, knowing my downside, knowing the worst case scenario and knowing, hey, I'm going to make money in this market. This was where I made my mark. This is where I strike because I had that knowingness. I knew what I was going to do this time. This time, I was going to put my money where the property was. I knew what a good deal looked like and I knew how to avoid the bad deals. All I had to do now was find the right opportunity and get it done. Within one year of moving to San Diego, I had bought my first multifamily unit, 48 units. I was as nervous as I was excited for the opportunity. 30 days after that purchase, I bought 38 more units in Point Loma. So while I look back on my career and say the first three years, all I did was shop. All I did was waste brokers time. All I did was waste my time. I didn't buy anything. And by the way, I missed millions of dollars of opportunities. The reality is in the first year that I was in San Diego, all that three years of research accumulated in a 48, a 38, and then a 92 unit deal. I literally had over 150 units in a period of 12 months. And I needed those three years. I needed those three years of research in Houston. Three years after hitting the West Coast, I had over 500 units to my name. It was staggering and a complete departure from my total stallout in Houston. That was 25 years ago. To this day, I anchor my business in the two things I know above all else. One, apartments are the best place for me to put my money. Number two, I know my markets. I invest a lot of time, resources, and research in understanding the market that I'm buying in so I can make the most of Point Number One. I want to know everything. I want to have control. I want to be able to determine the outcome before I invest. This is not speculation. This is good, smart investing. And when I say I want to know everything, I mean I want to know everything. I want to know where the jobs are, where the jobs are going, or jobs coming in. Is the economy growing or is it slowing? By the way, a slowing job market is not a bad place to invest in. I want to understand the politics of the market. I want to know how politics impact a business and the residents. I want to know about the rents in the market, the incomes in the market, occupancies, concessions,

## [06:02 - 06:59]

the credit worthiness of the existing and new tenants. I need to wrap my head around, and you do need to wrap your head around the competition across the street, down the block, and the history of the property you're buying in the neighborhood. You have to have a clear-cut view of the last buyer. Why did they fail? Why are they selling? Why are they ready to go? What did they pay? How did they manage? I need to have a grasp on the property manager's work ethic and their reputation. I want to feel good when I walk back on the property day and night. Whether I'm in Turks and Caicos or whether I'm on the property, I want to be a proud landlord, not a slumlord. I want to know. I don't want to guess. Listen to me. I want to know. You must know. Don't guess. Invest. Don't speculate. You should feel this way when you set out to find your next or your first deal. You must know. It's a great deal when you go in.
